title: practical english usage - Part 265
content:
Note how  is and are can often be used interchangeably.

total:    forty-one thousand and twenty-six.

two and one are three and one are four.

and one are ten and one is eleven; put down one and carry one; one is nine and one is ten; put down nought and carry one; nine twelve; put down two and carry one; six and two are eight and addition:   Six and nought and nought is six; eight and four and nought is next line:  One times 146 is 146.

down six and carry three; eight ones are eight and three is eleven.

and carry four; eight fours are thirty-two and four is thirty-six; put next line:  Put down one nought. Eight sixes are forty-eight; put down eight carry one; two fours are eight and one are nine; two ones are two.

beginning: Put down two noughts. Two sixes are twelve; put down two and A hundred and forty-six times two hundred and eighty-one.

out on paper before the days of pocket calculators.

words that a British English speaker might have used as he/she was working it Here, for interest, is a multiplication (146x281) together with all its steps, in the 22 example       of  a  spoken      calculation Nine divided by three equals/is three, (formal) 9 + 3  =  3        Three(s) into nine goes three (times), (informal, especially BrE) Three multiplied by four equals/is twelve, (formal) Three times four is twelve, (informal) 3  x 4 = 12      Three fours are twelve, (informal) Seven minus  four equals/is three, (formal) Seven take away four is/leaves three, (informal) 7-4  = 3        Four from seven is/leaves three, (informal) Two plus two equals/is four, (formal) 2 + 2 =  4       Two and two is/are four, (informal) Common ways of saying calculations in British English are: spoken      calculations There are twelve of us in my family. (More natural than We are twelve...) not I've got three sisters, (     My sisters are three.) be except in calculations, ►    322.21 below.

Numbers are used as subjects or objects, but not usually as complements after 20 numbers       not   used    as  complements          after    be Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary •  323    talking about age For the position of dates in letters, ►     289.   For full stops in abbreviations, ►    336.

(less commonly 1960's).

'fhe names of decades (e.g. the nineteen sixties) can be written like this: the 1960s Jan Feb Mar Apr Aug  Sept Oct Nov Dec The  longer names of the months are often abbreviated as follows: example, 6.4.02 means '6 April 2002' in Britain, but ‘ June 4, 2002’  in the USA.

people put the day first while Americans generally start with the month. So for All-figure dates are written differently in Britain and America, since British March 30, 2004 the year.

In the USA it is common to write the month first and to put a comma  before 30/3/04  30-3-04  30.3.04 The  date may be written entirely in figures.

He was born in Hawick on 14 December, 1942.

common in Britain except when the date comes inside a sentence.

3rd, 6th). Some people write a comma  before the year, but this is no longer very The  last two letters of the number word are sometimes added (e.g. 1st, 2nd, 30 March 2004  27 July 2003 names of months always begin with capital letters (►  341).

In Britain, the commonest way to write the day's date is as follows. Note that the 1  writing 324dates At your age I already had a job.

He could read at the age of three, (     .. ,-in-the-age-...) In other structures, at is common before age.

The two boys are the same age. She's the same age as me.

When I was your age I was working, (     When I was at your age...) Note the structure be + ... age (without a preposition).

We   ask How old are you?, not normally What is your age?

He is thirty years old / of age. (     ... thirty years.) or be + number + years old (more formal:. .. of age).

He is thirty, (     He has thirty.) We most often talk about people's ages with be + number 1  use   of  be 323 ta lking    about     age  _____________________ vocabulary •  325  telling the time OK,  see you at half two. (= . .. half past two.) Past is often dropped from half past in informal speech.
